coredata.dc:title: Sugar derived-fluorescent carbon quantum dots conjugated glutathione for sensing heavy metal ions and antioxidant activity
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chobpattana, Varistha
Sangtawesin, Tanagorn
Khaopueak, Pacharaphon
Wechakorn, Kanokorn
coredata.dc:description: Heavy metal ions, including Hg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, and As5+, can induce oxidative stress, damage organs, and disrupt biological processes, leading to serious health hazards. This research aims to develop a fluorescent sensor based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to detect these metal ions. CQDsfrom table sugarwere synthesized via gamma irradiationat ambient temperature without the use of catalysts, toxic chemicals, and hazardous wastes. The chemical properties of the CQDs, including particle sizes, surface functional groups, and chemical bonding, were thoroughly investigated. Glutathione groups (GSH) were functionalized on the CQD’s surface. In the presence of heavy metal ions, such as Hg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, and As5+, the “turn on” fluorescence of CQDs-GSH was quenched in proportion to the increasing concentrations, enabling the sensor to monitor these ions. The sensitivity, selectivity, and stability ofCQDs-GSHwere also evaluated. Additionally,CQDs-GSHexhibited notable antioxidant activity usinga DPPH radical scavenging assay.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Heavy metal ion
Fluorescent sensor
Gamma radiation
Table sugar
Glutathione

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots conjugated glutathione (CQDs-GSH)
Fluorescent sensors for heavy metal ion detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by a research funding project for young scientist researchers, the Coordinating Center for Thai Government Science and Technology Scholarship Students (CSTS) and the Science, Research and Innovation Promotion Fund, Thailand Science Research and Innovation (TSRI), through Rajamangala University of Technology Thanyaburi ( FRB67E0610 ) (Grant No.: FRB670027/0168 ). We would like to thank the Thailand Institute of Nuclear Technology (Public Organization) and the Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology , Rajamangala University of Technology Thanyaburi , for facility support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) size-distribution diagram, and (c) XRD pattern of CQDs.
(a) XPS survey and high resolution spectra of (b) C1s, (c) O1s, and (d) N1s of CQDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption, (b) fluorescence emission spectra, (c) normalized fluorescence intensity of CQDs-GSH (0.2 mg/mL) in deionized water, the quantum yield of (d) quinine hemisulfate monohydrate and (e) CQDs-GSH, and (f) the relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0) of CQDs-GSH in the presence of NaCl (0 − 1 M).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra, (b) fluorescence intensity (λem = 355 nm), and (c) photographs under UV illumination at 365 nm of CQDs-GSH (0.2 mg/mL) in the presence of various metal ions (2.0 mM).

Fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs-GSH (0.2 mg/mL) in the addition of (a) Na2HAsO4, (c) Fe(OAc)2, (e) FeCl3⋅6H2O, (g) Hg(OAc)2 and the linear plots of (F0 – F)/F0 in the presence of (b) Na2HAsO4 (0.09–0.19 mM), (d) Fe(OAc)2 (0.01–0.8 mM), (f) FeCl3⋅6H2O (0.01–0.9 mM), and (h) Hg(OAc)2 (0.04–0.9 mM).
(a) Absorption spectra and (b) % DPPH inhibition of CQDs-GSH (0 − 1 mg/mL).
Zeta potentials of the CQDs-GSH (0.1 mg/mL) with and without various metal ions.

head.ce:title: Sugar derived-fluorescent carbon quantum dots conjugated glutathione for sensing heavy metal ions and antioxidant activity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Rajamangala University of Technology Thanyaburi, Pathum Thani 12110, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Photochemical and Electrochemical Materials Research Unit, Faculty of Science and Technology, Rajamangala University of Technology Thanyaburi, Pathum Thani 12110, Thailand

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Rajamangala University of Technology Thanyaburi, Pathum Thani 12110, Thailand.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heavy metal ions, including Hg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, and As5+, can induce oxidative stress, damage organs, and disrupt biological processes, leading to serious health hazards. This research aims to develop a fluorescent sensor based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to detect these metal ions. CQDsfrom table sugarwere synthesized via gamma irradiationat ambient temperature without the use of catalysts, toxic chemicals, and hazardous wastes. The chemical properties of the CQDs, including particle sizes, surface functional groups, and chemical bonding, were thoroughly investigated. Glutathione groups (GSH) were functionalized on the CQD’s surface. In the presence of heavy metal ions, such as Hg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, and As5+, the “turn on” fluorescence of CQDs-GSH was quenched in proportion to the increasing concentrations, enabling the sensor to monitor these ions. The sensitivity, selectivity, and stability ofCQDs-GSHwere also evaluated. Additionally,CQDs-GSHexhibited notable antioxidant activity usinga DPPH radical scavenging assay.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury (II) ion (Hg2+) is highly toxic and poses significant health and environmental risks. In aquatic environments, Hg2+ converts to methylmercury, which bioaccumulates in fish and seafood [1]. This leads to higher concentrations throughout the food chain, posing risks to both wildlife and humans. Hg2+ is neurotoxic and causes extensive damage to the central and peripheral nervous systems [2]. Symptoms include tremors, memory loss, cognitive deficits, and motor dysfunction. Additionally, the mercury accumulation can cause kidney and liver damage [3].

Iron ions, while essential for various biological processes, can become toxic to humans and the environment when present in excess. For instance, excess iron catalyzes the Fenton reaction, leading to the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as hydroxyl radicals, which cause oxidative stress and damage lipids, proteins, and DNA [4]. Iron-induced ROS triggers lipid peroxidation, damaging cell membranes and leading to cell death [5,6]. In addition, excess iron ions can result in metabolic disturbances, neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, and Huntington's, as well as organ damage, including to the liver, heart, and pancreas [7].

During the process of clean water treatment, there is a possibility of heavy metal contamination, with arsenic being one of the most common types of contamination of heavy metals in the environment [8]. Arsenic in water can be found in both organic and inorganic forms, such as arsenite (As3+), arsenate (As5+), monomethyl-arsenic acid, and dimethyl-arsenic acid [9]. It is known to be carcinogenic, which can cause respiratory symptoms as well as increase the risk of skin cancer [10].

Regarding qualitative and quantitative analysis, heavy metal ions can be analyzed using ion chromatography, atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), or inductively coupled plasma-mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS) [11]. Even though these techniques offer high accuracy, individual spectroscopy specialists are needed. In addition, the high cost of the instruments, complex analysis, and long analysis time can hinder the investigations [12]. Concurrently, nanotechnology has been drawn upon in many fields, including biology, chemistry, and physics. The development of chemical sensors was designed with many nanomaterials for analyzing metal ions [13]. Carbon dots (CDs) are nanoscale particles which have been receiving extensive attention since 2006 attributing to their environmental friendliness [14]. In addition to their non-toxicity, they are soluble in water, optically stable, biocompatible, and economically friendly [15]. They usually show notable fluorescence characteristics and high chemical stability [16]. Furthermore, carbon dots (CDs) exhibit notable antioxidant activity due to their unique properties and functional groups [17]. Functional groups on the surface of CDs, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups, are essential to their antioxidant potential by interacting with reactive oxygen species and neutralizing them [18]. CDs can protect cells from oxidative stress-induced damage, making them useful in biomedical applications, such as drug delivery, bioimaging, and biosensing [19]. Moreover, CDs can be incorporated into food packaging materials to extend shelf life by reducing oxidation and spoilage [20].

Various CDs have been synthesized via top-down and bottom-up methods such as hydrothermal, microwave irradiation, arc-discharge, laser ablation, electrochemical, or ultrasonic treatment [21,22]. One of the alternative methods is gamma radiation. Gamma ray is electromagnetic with a wavelength shorter than X-ray, in the range between 10−20 to 10−22 m or shorter than 10−20 m. It emits high-energy photons at a short wavelength. Gamma radiation permits chemical reactions at low temperatures without any catalyst. The process reduces pollution since there is little to no chemical involvement. In addition, the process does not affect the physical appearance of the material. When the high-energy photons reach the material (e.g., polymers or nanocarbons), there are several complex reactions that occur, including structural changes (oxidations, reductions, or cleavage), bond dissociation, reactive oxygen species, polymerization, or chain cleavages [23]. Recently, CQDs from sugar were successfully prepared by gamma irradiation, displaying strong UV absorption and excitation-dependent fluorescence emission [24].

Table sugar (sucrose) is a typical disaccharide comprised of glucose and fructose molecules connected by a glycosidic bond. Table sugar was a desirable precursor for CQD production because it was achievable, inexpensive for large-scale production, had high carbon and oxygen content, excellent water solubility, and non-toxicity [25]. Table sugar-derived CQDs have distinct optical and electrical properties, enabling them to be utilized in a variety of applications, including reducing agents, sensors, and biological applications [26,27].

This research focuses on developing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from sugar via gamma radiation as a nano-chemical sensor for heavy metal ion detections in water. This environmentally friendly process incorporates sugar/sucrose, which is a sweet, short-chain, water-soluble carbohydrate. The surface functionalization of CQDs was designed by conjugating glutathione (GSH) to create thiol and oxygen functional groups, which reacted with heavy metal ions. GSH is a tripeptide composed of three amino acids: glutamine, cysteine, and glycine. It has been considered a prevalent biomarker in medical diagnosis and monitoring [28]. In addition, it was proposed that GSH molecules could isolate gold nanoparticles due to their steric structure, which “turn on” the fluorescence emission of carbon dots [28]. Fluorescence quenching was distinguishably observed in the presence of Hg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, and As5+. The absorption and fluorescence emission properties of the as-synthesized CQDs-GSH were analyzed, including the sensitivity, selectivity, limit of detection, and stability. Furthermore, the antioxidant activity of CQDs-conjugated glutathione was tested.

All of the utilized compounds were of analytical grade and required no additional purification. Sugar (Mitrphol) was purchased from a commercial supplier. Glutathione was purchased from Acros. Cu(OAc)2⋅H2O, Zn(OAc)2, Ce(OAc)3⋅H2O, quinine hemisulfate monohydrate, and 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Pb(OAc)2⋅3H2O, Cu(OAc)2⋅H2O, Zn(OAc)2, Co(OAc)2⋅4H2O, Mg(OAc)2⋅4H2O, and FeCl3⋅6H2O were purchased from Alfa Aesar. Cd(OAc)2⋅2H2O, Ca(OAc)2⋅H2O, Fe(OAc)2, and Ni(OAc)2⋅4H2O were purchased from Acros Organics. As2O3 and Na2HAsO4⋅7H2O were purchased from Ajax Finechem. Hg(OAc)2 was purchased from QReC. 30 % NH3 was purchased from Panreac Applichem. Deionized water (R > 18 M Ω cm−1) was used in all experiments.

Gamma radiation was performed by cobalt-60, JS 8900 IR-155 (Nordion). The zeta potential was analyzed by Malvern Panalytical Zetasizer Ultra. Fourier-transformed infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were measured using Thermo Scientific Nicolet is5/iD7 ATR. UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a JASCO V-570 UV/Visible spectrometer. Fluorescence emission spectra were measured on a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer. X-ray diffraction spectrum (XRD) was recorded by PAN analytical X’Pert PRO MPD model PW 3040/60 diffractometer. X-ray photoelectron (XPS) spectra were measured by Kratos AXIS Ultra DLD (Shimadzu). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were explored on the Thermo Scientific TALOS F200X.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots conjugated glutathione (CQDs-GSH)
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs were prepared from table sugar, according to the previous literature [24]. 20.0 g of sugar was heated at 100 °C until it melted and turned brown. Then, 200 mL of 12.5 % NH3 solution was drop-wise added to the melted sugar while stirring for 2 h. To eliminate oxygen gas, the brown solution (10 % w/v) was transferred to glass vessels and purged with nitrogen gas for 30 min. After that, the mixture was radiated with gamma rays at 25 kGy. Consequently, the sample underwent a dialysis process in deionized water for 24 h, with deionized water replaced every 3 h. In addition, the yellow solution was freeze-dried to achieve carbon quantum dots (CQDs).

To prepare CQDs conjugated with glutathione (GSH), 10.0 mg of CQDs was dissolved in 3.0 mL of deionized water. Then, the sample was sonicated for 30 min. Concurrently, 2.0 mg of GSH was dissolved in 2.0 mL of distilled water. Finally, the solution of GSH was mixed with the CQDs, continuously stirring for 1 h.

Selectivity of CQDs-GSH towards various metal ions, including Na2HAsO4⋅7H2O, Zn(OAc)2, Ce(OAc)3⋅H2O, Pb(OAc)2⋅3H2O, Cu(OAc)2⋅H2O, Zn(OAc)2, Co(OAc)2⋅4H2O, Mg(OAc)2⋅4H2O, Hg(OAc)2, FeCl3⋅6H2O, Cd(OAc)2⋅2H2O, Ca(OAc)2⋅H2O, Fe(OAc)2 and Ni(OAc)2⋅4H2O, was investigated using fluorescence emission by an excitation wavelength of 300 nm. For the selectivity experiment, the fluorescence emission of CQDs-GSH (0.2 mg/mL) in the addition of various metal ions (2.0 mM) was performed in the deionized water. To study other ion interferences, the fluorescence emission of CQDs-GSH (0.2 mg/mL) in the addition of Hg(OAc)2 and various metal ions (2.0 mM) was elucidated in the deionized water.

The fluorescence quantum yield (Φ) of CQDs-GSH was investigated in the concentration range of 0.08 − 0.18 mg/mL. The Φ of the sample (X) was compared to quinine hemisulfate monohydrate (
Φ
ST
= 54 %) using the slope of the absorbance and the integrated fluorescence intensity curve according to the following equation [20],
Φ x
=
Φ
ST
Slope
x
Slope
ST
η
x
2
η
ST
2
where η is the refractive index of the solvent.

Fluorescence titration experiments were used to determine the limit of detection (LOD). Fluorescence intensity of the CQDs-GSH solution without metal ion addition was used as a blank measurement to calculate the standard deviation (σ), and m is the slope of the linear calibration curve of (F0 – F)/F0 versus the concentrations of Na2HAsO4, Fe(OAc)2, Hg(OAc)2, and FeCl3⋅6H2O. The limit of detection was calculated as the following equation:
L O D
=
3.3 σ
m

Using the DPPH radical scavenging assay, the antioxidant activity of CQDs-GSH (0–1.0 mg/mL) was examined [29]. The CQDs-GSH solution (500 μL) was mixed with a stock solution of DPPH in MeOH (1.0 mM, 250 μL) and 50 % MeOH/H2O (250 μL). The resulting reaction was treated at room temperature for 1 h in the dark. A UV–visible spectrophotometer was used to measure the absorbance at 550 nm. Using the following formula, the proportion of DPPH inhibition by CQDs-GSH (Asample) compared to the DPPH solution (Acontrol) was determined:‬‬‬
% DPPH
inhibition
activity =
A control
-
A sample
A control
×
100

CQDs from table sugar were green synthesized using gamma irradiation of a 25 kGy dose. The particle morphology and size distribution of CQDs were determined by TEM analysis. CQDs showed a uniformly spherical shape with monodispersed (Fig. 1 a). The average diameter of CQDs was found to be 75 ± 10 nm in the range of 57–103 nm from the size distribution diagram (Fig. 1b). The crystalline structure of CQDs was also determined by X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (Fig. 1c). The XRD diffractogram of CQDs shows the broad band centered at 21.5°, describing the 002 planes of graphite characteristics [30]. The amorphous nature of CQDs was suggested by the oxygen-containing functionalities, as evidenced by the broad peak and relatively high interlayer distance value of 0.41 nm [30].

CQDs functionalized glutathione (CQDs-GSH) were prepared by mixing the solution of CQDs with glutathione. The zeta potentials of CQDs and CQDs-GSH were determined using dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements (Fig. S1). The zeta potential of CQDs was found to be −36.4 ± 3.1 mV, less than that of CQDs-GSH (−12.1 ± 0.9 mV), suggesting that the hydroxyl groups of CQDs could chelate the amino acids of glutathione via intermolecular hydrogen bonding, leading to glutathione on the surface of CQDs [32].

The CQDs-GSH solution absorbs UV–visible light, showing strong characteristic absorption peaks centered at 225 and 300 nm, which were assigned to glutathione [33] and the π → π* transition of sp2 -hybridized carbon conjugation for graphene quantum dots, respectively (Fig. 4 a) [34]. The η → π* transition is a characteristic of the absorption shoulder of CQDs close to 400 nm [34]. The fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs-GSH, excited between 240 and 500 nm, showed red-shift fluorescence emission wavelengths from 360 to 540 nm and the highest emission wavelength at 355 nm, with the excitation at 300 nm (Fig. 4b, c). The tunable fluorescence emission depended on the excitation wavelength due to the different sizes and different surface functional groups of CQDs-GSH [31]. The quantum yield (Φ) of CQDs-GSH was determined to be 22.85 ± 0.04 %, compared to quinine hemisulfate monohydrate as the standard reference (Φ = 54 %) (Fig. 4d, e). Furthermore, the fluorescence emission of the CQDs-GSH solution in the presence of high salt concentration was investigated in sodium chloride (Fig. 4f). The fluorescence intensity of CQDs-GSH in the addition of high salt concentration up to 1.0 M was higher than 89 %, suggesting that CQDs-GSH exhibited high stability in a strong ionic environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent sensors for heavy metal ion detection
ce:section.ce:para:
In addition, the fluorescence emission of CQDs-GSH in the presence of these ions, including Na2HAsO4·7H2O, Fe(OAc)2, Hg(OAc)2, and FeCl3⋅6H2O was investigated in the range of 0 − 5.0 mM (Fig. 6 ). The fluorescence intensity of CQDs-GSH decreased with the increasing concentrations of these ions. In the presence of Na2HAsO4, Fe(OAc)2, Hg(OAc)2, and FeCl3⋅6H2O, the good linear relationships of the relative fluorescence intensity ([F0–F]/F0) were observed in the range of 0.09–0.19 mM (R
2 =0.9969), 0.01–0.8 mM (R
2 =0.9966), 0.04–0.9 mM (R
2 =0.9962), and 0.01–0.9 mM (R
2 =0.9967), respectively. For detecting these ions, such as As5+, Fe2+, Hg2+, and Fe3+, the limit of detections (LOD) were calculated to be 31.5, 122.4, 96.4, and 161.9 μM, respectively. The results indicate that CQDs-GSH could be further applied as a fluorescent sensor to monitor these ions in environmental and industrial water contamination.

We investigated the antioxidant properties of CQDs-GSH using a colorimetric technique that scavenges DPPH radicals (Fig. 7 ). In the presence of antioxidant activity, the DPPH solution changes to a non-radical form of the DPPH-H complex in the DPPH free radical scavenging mechanism [18]. Greater DPPH inhibitory capabilities were demonstrated by increasing CQDs-GSH concentrations, as evidenced by reduced absorbance at 550 nm. At a 1.0 mg/mL dosage, CQDs-GSH demonstrated a 60 % DPPH radical scavenging activity. Higher scavenging activity indicates stronger antioxidant properties.

In summary, we demonstrated gamma irradiation for the synthesis of stable CQDs with hydroxyl groups conjugated to an sp2/sp3-hybridized carbon framework from table sugar. The enhanced properties of CQDs-GSH were effectively utilized for the detection of Hg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, and As5+. A good linear relationship of fluorescence against metal ion solutions was determined in the range of 0.01–0.19 mM, with an R2 value between 0.9962–0.9969. In addition, CQDs-GSH exhibited strong antioxidant activity due to their ability to scavenge reactive oxygen species and the presence of functional groups on their surface. This development makes them valuable in a range of applications, from biomedical to environmental protection.

Varistha Chobpattana: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Resources, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Tanagorn Sangtawesin: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation. Pacharaphon Khaopueak: Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis. Kanokorn Wechakorn: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by a research funding project for young scientist researchers, the Coordinating Center for Thai Government Science and Technology Scholarship Students (CSTS) and the Science, Research and Innovation Promotion Fund, Thailand Science Research and Innovation (TSRI), through Rajamangala University of Technology Thanyaburi (FRB67E0610) (Grant No.: FRB670027/0168). We would like to thank the Thailand Institute of Nuclear Technology (Public Organization) and the Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Rajamangala University of Technology Thanyaburi, for facility support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mseb.2024.117956.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
